frameworks.name: robust averaging
frameworks.one_line: human observers downweight outlying features when averaging visual information

frameworks.name: ideal observer
frameworks.one_line: equal weighting of equally reliable sources as the optimality benchmark

frameworks.name: late noise integration model
frameworks.one_line: computational account where decision noise corrupts integration after encoding

core_question: Why do humans discount outlying features when averaging visual information instead of weighting all reliable sources equally?
key_assumptions: an ideal observer gives equal weight to equally reliable sources
averaging-style perceptual decisions can be modeled as feature integration
noise can arise during integration, not only at encoding
manipulations: tilt of individual gratings in a circular array relative to a central reference
presence and magnitude of outlying / deviant feature values
key_findings: observers downweighted deviant orientations when judging average tilt
degree of robust averaging positively predicted overall task performance
simulations: robust averaging is suboptimal for a perfect integrator
simulations: robust averaging improves performance when late noise is present
interpretations: discarding outlying decision information is a strategy for resilience against noise in neural computations
apparent suboptimality of human averaging is adaptive given internal decision noise
